request.question: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?

title: Giuseppe Farina
Jeff Wood (racing driver)
Olson (constructor)
Snowberger
Wetteroth
Charles Van Acker
Langley (constructor)
Andy Michner
Josele Garza
1950 Indianapolis 500
sentences: Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.
He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.
During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.
Jeff Wood (born January 20, 1957) is an American former race car driver born in Wichita, Kansas.
He was Formula Atlantic Rookie of the Year in 1977.
He drove in the CanAm series for Carl Haas in 1981 and finished 5th in the series behind Danny Sullivan.
In 1982, driving for Bob Garnetson Racing, finished 3rd place in the 24 Hours of Daytona.
He then made his CART debut in 1983 at the Caesars Palace Grand Prix driving for Dan Gurney.
He then drove in the Formula Atlantic West Series where he won the 1985 championship.
He returned to CART in 1987 and made 4 starts for Dick Simon with his best finish being 10th.
He was away from the series in 1988, then returned in 1989 to make 5 starts for Gohr Racing, where he finished 12th at the Michigan 500.
In 1990 he drove for a new team, Todd Walther Racing, where he made his first attempt to qualify for the Indianapolis 500, but crashed in practice.
He made 10 other starts, with a 10th-place finish at Phoenix, finishing 22nd in points.
In 1991 he made 8 starts for Dale Coyne Racing, Arciero Racing, and Euromotorsports with the best finish being 13th.
7 starts in '92 for Arciero netted Wood a 12th-place finish and a single point.
In 1993 Wood attempted a nearly full season for Andrea Moda Formula/Euromotorsports but failed to qualify for 6 races and only made 8 starts and again failed to score points.
He made four more unsuccessful starts in 1994 for Euromotorsports in what would be his final races in the series.
He was named to an entry for the 1996 Indianapolis 500, but the car failed to appear.
His best finish in his 49 CART races was an 8th place that came in his second series start back in 1983 at Laguna Seca Raceway.
Olson was a racing car constructor.
Olson cars competed in one round of the FIA World Championship - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Snowberger was a racing car constructor.
Snowberger cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Wetteroth was a racing car constructor.
Wetteroth cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.
He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.
In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.
He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.
In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.
1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.
He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.
Langley was an American racing car constructor.
Langley cars competed in one FIA World Championship race - the 1950 Indianapolis 500.
Andy Michner (born October 27, 1968, Ann Arbor, Michigan), is a former driver in the Indy Racing League IndyCar Series, NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series and NASCAR Busch Series.
He is the current record holder of the world's fastest Sprint Car race at a United States Auto Club event in Phoenix, Arizona at 136.034 mph 1996.
Michner finished twice a runner-up to NASCAR'S Tony Stewart in United States Auto Club competition and has 19 USAC Wins.
He passed his Indy 500 Rookie Orientation Program but elected to not qualify for the race 1996 Indianapolis 500.
In 1996 & 1997, Michner ran a partial season in the NASCAR Craftsman Truck Series as Chevrolet's Development Driver.
He then returned to the Indy Car series in 1998 with Konica/Syan Racing and captured his career best finish of eighth place in his first race, the 1998 Indianapolis 500.
Michner then signed with Factory Riley&Scott Reebok Indycar Team where Michner led in the closing laps of the 1998 Texas Longhorn 500 but failed to finish due to an engine failure.
At Michigan International Speedway in August 1998, it was announced, Michner signed a 3-year contract to drive the Bayer Aleve, Coca-Cola Chevrolet in NASCAR's Busch Series for BACE Motorsports.
Michner suffered ultimately career ending injuries in October 1998 while testing at Homestead-Miami Speedway in a NASCAR Busch Series test.
He attempted to qualify for the 1999 Indianapolis 500 for Byrd Racing but failed to make the field due to rain.
He was named to a Logan Racing entry to two races in 2000 but the car did not appear at either race.
Josele Garza (born March 15, 1962 in Mexico City, Mexico) is a Mexican professional race car driver.
He started seven Indianapolis 500 races.
Garza was the 1981 Indianapolis 500 Rookie of the Year after rolling off sixth and finishing 23rd at the age of 19.
At 19 years, two months, and nine days, Garza is the second youngest driver to compete in the Indianapolis 500 and was not surpassed until 2003 by A.J. Foyt IV.
USAC rules at the time required drivers to be 21 years of age, but Garza's racing license listed him as being 22 years old.
Garza claims he does not know how the error occurred but he did not notify officials of the error.
He finished 10th in the 1984 Indianapolis 500.
He led 13 Indy 500 laps.
The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.
The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.
It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.
The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.
Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.
The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What year was the man whose car failed to arrive to the 1950 Indianapolis 500 first the Italian champion?
hop1_docs: 1950 Indianapolis 500 | The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.  The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.  It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.  The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.  Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.  The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

2001 Indianapolis 500 | The 85th Indianapolis 500 was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway in Speedway, Indiana on Sunday, May 27, 2001.  Race rookie Hélio Castroneves, a three-year veteran of the CART series, led the final 52 laps and won his first Indy 500.  Penske Racing swept 1st-2nd with Gil de Ferran the runner-up.  Winning car owner Roger Penske scored his 11th victory at the Indianapolis 500, and his first-ever 1-2 finish in the race.  It was a redemption from the team's previous attempt at Indy (1995) in which both of his cars failed to qualify; subsequently followed by a five-year absence (1996-2000) due to the open wheel "split."

Chuck Ciprich | Charles J. Ciprich (Born November 30, 1941) is an American former racing driver from Sayre, Pennsylvania.  A successful modified stock car racer who won numerous championships throughout the northeast, Ciprich tried his hand at Championship Car racing in 1983 and 1984.  He first attempted to qualify for the 1983 Indianapolis 500 in an unsophisticated Chevrolet powered Finley chassis fielded by Pace Racing.  He made his CART Champ Car debut later that year at Pocono Raceway but qualified near the back of the field and was knocked out after 38 laps by a transmission failure.  Ciprich and the Pace team arrived for the first oval race of the 1984 season at Phoenix International Raceway with a more advanced, yet year-old March-Cosworth combination yet failed to make the field.  Their attempt to qualify the car for the 1984 Indianapolis 500 was equally unsuccessful and Ciprich returned to Modifieds.  Ciprich retired some years later and now resides in Florida.

Charles Van Acker | Charles Van Acker (14 March 1912 Brussels – 31 May 1998 South Bend, Indiana) was a Belgian-American racecar driver.  He first attempted to qualify for the Indianapolis 500 in 1946 but was too slow.  In 1947 he made the race and finished in 29th after a crash on lap 24.  He also competed in seven more races of the national trail that season and finished 4th in points.  In 1948 he 11th in the Indy 500 and 10th in the National Championship.  1949 saw him crash 10 laps into the Indy 500 and struggle to qualify much of the rest of the season.  He attempted the 1950 Indianapolis 500 but failed to qualify in what would be his last Championship Car appearance.

Bob Frey | Robert C. Frey (born October 25, 1950) is a former American racing driver from Elyria, Ohio.  He was a notable sprint car driver who won some of the country's most prestigious races when he attempted to try his hand at CART Championship Car racing in 1980.  His first attempt to qualify for a race at the Pocono Raceway ended in a practice crash.  His first race came in September of that year at the Michigan International Speedway and he finished 17th.  The following year Frey notched his best CART finish of 13th at Phoenix International Raceway but then failed to qualify his eight-year-old Eagle-Offy for the 1981 Indianapolis 500.  He returned the week after the Indy 500 and drove in the Milwaukee Mile race, finishing 17th.  Frey returned in 1982 with a much newer 1981 Eagle chassis but failed to qualify for both the Indy 500 and the Milwaukee race, after which he returned to sprint cars.

1978 USAC Championship Car season | The 1978 USAC Championship Car season consisted of 18 races, beginning at Phoenix on March 18 and concluding at the same location on October 28.  The USAC National Champion was Tom Sneva and the Indianapolis 500 winner was Al Unser.  This was the last year before the first USAC/CART "Split".  By winning the Indianapolis 500, Pocono 500, and California 500, Al Unser swept the Indy car "Triple Crown", the only driver in history to do so.

Phil Caliva | Phil Caliva (born January 1, 1945 in Palermo, Sicily) is an Italian-born American racing driver who competed in the CART Championship Car series from 1979 to 1984.  After racing in the North American Formula Super Vee series in 1977 and 1978, he made his CART debut in 1979 driving for Spike Gehlhausen at Ontario Motor Speedway.  Caliva returned the next year and made five starts for Bill Alsup and tried but failed to qualify for the Indianapolis 500.  Caliva attempted a second time to qualify for the "500" for Alsup in 1981 but again failed to make the field.  He made 3 more starts later that year, including his best series finish of 8th place at the Michigan International Speedway in July.  He tried 3 races including the Indy 500 in 1982 but failed to qualify for all of them.  He tried the Indy 500 again in 1983 and missed the field again, but made the race at Riverside International Raceway in what would be his last Champ Car start.  He tried the Long Beach race and the Indy 500 again in 1984 but again missed both races.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Giuseppe Farina — passage [1]: "Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived."
  - No passages mention any championship year for Farina.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Ambiguity in phrasing: "first the Italian champion" could mean (a) the first year Farina became an Italian (national) champion, (b) the year he first became a champion (e.g., World Drivers' Champion), or (c) the year he became the first Italian to win a specific championship.
  - No candidate other than Farina is present.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The specific championship intended and the year Giuseppe Farina first held that title (e.g., year he first became World Drivers' Champion or year he first won an Italian national championship).

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Giuseppe Farina — full biography page (e.g., "Giuseppe Farina" Wikipedia article).
  2. "1950 World Drivers' Championship" or "1950 Formula One World Championship" page to confirm championship winner and year.
  3. If intent is a national Italian championship, search: "Giuseppe Farina Italian champion year" or "Giuseppe Farina national championships".
response.hop2_query: Giuseppe Farina World Drivers' Championship 1950 first champion
hop2_docs: Giuseppe Farina | Dottore Emilio Giuseppe "Nino" Farina (30 October 1906 – 30 June 1966), was an Italian racing driver and was the first official Formula One World Champion, gaining the title in 1950.  He was also the Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.  During his thirty-year racing career he suffered a series of accidents.

Formula One drivers from Italy | There have been 98 Formula One drivers from Italy including two World Drivers' Champions.  Giuseppe "Nino" Farina won the first ever World Drivers' Championship and Alberto Ascari was the first double world champion.  All three championships came in the early 1950s and very few Italian drivers have come close since Ascari's 1953 victory.  Four of the most experienced Grand Prix drivers in history come from Italy.

1950 Italian Grand Prix | The 1950 Italian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 3 September 1950 at Monza.  It was race 7 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers.  In this race, Nino Farina became the first World Drivers' Champion, and the only driver to win the title in his home country.

1950 Indianapolis 500 | The 34th International 500-Mile Sweepstakes was held at the Indianapolis Motor Speedway on Tuesday, May 30, 1950.  The event was part of the 1950 AAA National Championship Trail.  It was also race 3 of 7 in the 1950 World Championship of Drivers and paid points towards the World Championship.  The event, however, did not attract any European entries for 1950.  Giuseppe Farina originally planned to enter, but his car never arrived.  The Indianapolis 500 would be included on the World Championship calendar through 1960.

2007 FIA Formula One World Championship | The 2007 FIA Formula One World Championship was the 61st season of FIA Formula One motor racing.  It featured the 2007 FIA Formula One World Championship, which began on 18 March and ended on 21 October after seventeen events.  The Drivers' Championship was won by Ferrari driver Kimi Räikkönen by one point at the final race of the season, making Räikkönen the third Finnish driver to take the title.  An appeal by McLaren regarding the legality of some cars in the final race could have altered the championship standings, but on 16 November, the appeal was reportedly rejected by the International Court of Appeal, confirming the championship results.  Räikkönen entered the final race in third position in the drivers' standings, but emerged as champion after the chequered flag, a feat first accomplished by Giuseppe Farina in .  It has since been accomplished again, by Sebastian Vettel, in .

1950 Formula One season | The 1950 Formula One season was the fourth season of the FIA's Formula One motor racing.  It featured the inaugural FIA World Championship of Drivers which commenced on 13 May and ended on 3 September, as well as a number of non-championship races.  The championship consisted of six Grand Prix races, each held in Europe and open to Formula One cars, plus the Indianapolis 500, which was run to AAA National Championship regulations.  Giuseppe Farina won the championship from Juan Manuel Fangio and Luigi Fagioli.

1950 Bari Grand Prix | The 1950 Bari Grand Prix was a non-championship Formula One motor race held on 9 July 1950 at the Lungomare Circuit, in Bari, Italy.  It was the sixth race of the 1950 Formula One season.  The 60-lap race was won by Alfa Romeo driver Giuseppe Farina.  Juan Manuel Fangio finished second, also in an Alfa Romeo, and Stirling Moss third in an HWM-Alta.
response.summary_2: 1937. Giuseppe Farina was Italian Champion in 1937, 1938 and 1939.
